title: 8 Advanced RAG Techniques & How to Implement Them - Intuz
snippet:
Web Analytics

# 8 Advanced RAG Techniques and How to Implement Them

Updated 6 Jan 2026

author

Kamal R

Advanced RAG Techniques 

Explore 8 advanced Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) techniques with clear implementation guidance. Learn how to improve LLM accuracy, reduce hallucinations, optimize retrieval, and build production-ready RAG systems that scale for real-world enterprise use cases.

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) enables Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate responses by drawing on internal knowledge rather than relying solely on training data. At its simplest, RAG retrieves similar document chunks and passes them to the model as context. [...] | System Layer | Basic RAG | Advanced RAG |
 --- 
| Retrieval Strategy | Single dense vector search over all documents | Hybrid and multi-path retrieval combining dense vectors, lexical search, filters, and graphs |
| Ranking Logic | Cosine similarity decides the final order | Cross-encoders and re-rankers reassess relevance before context injection |
| Query Handling | Raw user input sent directly to retrieval | Queries are rewritten, expanded, or decomposed before search |
| Chunking Method | Fixed-length text splits | Semantic and hierarchical chunking aligned to document structure |
| Context Assembly | Top K chunks appended until token limit | Context is compressed, pruned, and prioritized before generation | [...] ### 3. Multi-vector retrieval

You apply this technique when there’s a need to preserve fine-grained matching in long, structurally dense documents. Relevance is computed by late interaction between query and document tokens, rather than a single embedding per chunk.

At query time, you:

Only the highest-scoring results are then forwarded into re-ranking and context compression.

### 4. Query optimization

Raw user queries are rarely retrieval-ready. Many are vague, unspecified, overloaded, or written in business language that doesn’t match how your documents are structured. When you send these queries directly into retrieval, recall collapses before ranking even begins.

Query optimization helps you insert a processing layer ahead of retrieval using three strategies:

title: Understanding the Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) Pipeline
snippet: What it does: After the initial retrieval (and filtering), the pipeline may rerank the results or fuse multiple result sets to improve relevance. Reranking typically involves taking the top N retrieved chunks and then running a more sophisticated ranking algorithm (even a small ML model or cross-attention model) to resort them based on how likely they are to contain the answer. This secondary ranking can use deeper features or context overlap with the query that the initial search might not fully capture. Fusion, as it applies here, refers to merging results from different retrieval approaches (as in hybrid retrieval) or from multiple queries. For example, if separate queries were issued (say, one for keywords and one for vectors, or sub-queries for different aspects of a complex [...] What it does: This is the step where the LLM generates the answer (or output) using the assembled prompt. The user’s query plus the retrieved context (and any instructions) are fed into the language model, and the model produces a response in natural language. Internally, the LLM uses the provided context to inform its completion, ideally synthesizing the relevant pieces into a coherent answer. For example, if the query was “What is our refund policy?” and the context included an excerpt from the company policy document, the LLM will incorporate that excerpt to compose an answer about the refund timeline and conditions. This step is essentially the “generation” part of retrieval-augmented generation. It may involve special prompting techniques such as few-shot examples or a specific [...] known patterns, LLM for others, etc.). Skipping this step, however, risks feeding a poorly formed query into retrieval and getting garbage out.

title: Aman's AI Journal • Primers • Retrieval Augmented Generation
snippet:
### Ingest: Document Parsing, Chunking, and Indexing

The ingest stage is an offline (i.e., asynchronous) step, responsible for transforming raw source material into a structured, searchable representation. This stage typically runs offline or asynchronously and is mandatory for any RAG system that operates over proprietary or external corpora.

At the end of the ingest stage, the system has a searchable, semantically enriched corpus that can be efficiently queried.

### Query: Understanding and Normalizing User Intent

The query stage transforms raw user input into a normalized retrieval representation. This stage runs synchronously for every request and begins with the red input block. [...] ##### Approaches

###### Standard/Naive approach

In the context of RAG, the advantages and disadvantages of the three approaches are as follows:

Advantages:

Disadvantages:

###### Sentence-Window Retrieval / Small-to-Large Retrieval

Advantages:

###### Auto-merging Retriever / Hierarchical Retriever

The method will finally retrieve the parent chunk for better context.

Advantages:

Disadvantages:

###### Contextual Retrieval

By prepending contextual metadata to each document chunk before embedding, Contextual Retrieval significantly enhances search accuracy. This approach reduces failed retrievals by 49% and, when combined with re-ranking, by 67%. [...] The output of the query stage is one or more normalized, enriched query representations that maximize retrieval effectiveness while respecting safety and policy constraints.

### Retrieve: Candidate Selection and Ranking

The retrieve stage is the core grounding mechanism of the system. Its goal is to identify the most relevant and policy-compliant document chunks for a given query.

A well-designed retrieve stage ensures that only relevant, compliant, and well-ranked context is passed to the generative model.

### Generate: Response Synthesis and Validation

The generate stage produces the final answer by synthesizing retrieved context with the generative model.

title: RAG Techniques: Part 1 of 5— Implementing 5 Effective Methods | by Naveen Pandey | Artificial Intelligence in Plain English
snippet:
## Method 4: Fusion RAG

Fusion-RAG (Fusion-Based Retrieval-Augmented Generation) is an advanced version of RAG that combines information from multiple data sources or retrieval methods to create more accurate and complete responses. This fusion can happen during the retrieval of information (retrieval fusion) or while generating the final response (generation fusion).

Unlike traditional RAG, which uses a single source for retrieval, Fusion-RAG enhances the process by refining query generation and document ranking, resulting in more relevant and accurate answers. It is especially useful when knowledge is spread across different repositories or when one source alone cannot provide a high-quality response.

## How RAG-Fusion Works [...] after executing the above code we got this following response.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

## Method 2: Corrective Retrieval Augmented Generation (CRAG)

Corrective Retrieval Augmented Generation (CRAG) represents a significant breakthrough in improving the accuracy and reliability of RAG systems. This innovative approach tackles one of the biggest challenges in traditional RAG implementations: unreliable document retrieval that can lead to inaccurate responses. CRAG has demonstrated impressive results, showing up to 36.6% improvement in accuracy compared to standard RAG systems across various tests.

### Key Features and Benefits of CRAG: [...] After executing the above code we got this following response:

Press enter or click to view image in full size

### Key Features and Benefits

1. Self-Correction:

 Validates document relevance
 Transforms queries when needed
 Falls back to web search for gaps

2. Flexibility:

 Graph-based workflow
 Easy to modify or extend
 Conditional processing paths

3. Quality Control:

 Document relevance checking
 Multiple retrieval attempts
 Structured decision making

## Method 3: Speculative RAG

In Speculative RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation), the model starts creating a response right away by guessing what information it might retrieve. Once the actual information is fetched, the response is updated, checked, or improved. This method saves time while still giving accurate answers.

title: A guide on how retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) works
snippet:
## Final thoughts

A RAG system isn't a simple product or a single off-the-shelf tool. It's a complete, comprehensive system composed of various components. All these components must play their role for the system to be able to initially retrieve information and then generate a response. You need to carefully design each of the components, keeping in mind your needs and the specificities of your use case.

Merge can help you deploy complex architectures like RAG, as it enables your product to access normalized data across hundreds of customers’ applications by simply building to its Unified API.

Screenshot of Merge [...] #### Query expansion

Query expansion takes the original query and adds additional relevant keywords to it. These extra keywords expand the scope of the search and help retrieve relevant documents, which could have otherwise been omitted.

#### Filtering

Filtering specifies criteria that the documents must meet in order to include them in the context. This way, only the most relevant documents are selected.

### LLMs

LLMs are probably the most well-known component in a RAG approach. In essence, an LLM is just an ML model that has been trained on huge amounts of data, which is what the word large in its name refers to. [...] Because they haven't been pretrained on new data, they don't have knowledge of current events. They can also give inaccurate answers (i.e., hallucinate), and they have little or no knowledge specific to your organization.

Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) is a technique that can overcome these gaps by letting LLMs pull up-to-date, relevant information from internal or external sources.

With RAG, LLMs aren't limited to what they were originally trained on—they can access new data in real time, including news, domain-specific knowledge, or even private company data.

Here’s more on how RAG works and how it can help your organization leverage LLMs effectively.

 ]
)

retriever = index.as_retriever(filters=filters)

### What is Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG)?

Learn how Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) enhances large language models by integrating external data sources. [...] # Multimodal RAG: A Hands-On Guide to Learning from Documents

Learn to build a Multimodal RAG pipeline for a visual Q&A system on IKEA assembly instructions using the OpenAI API and ChromaDB vector database.

Jan 13, 2026  · 11 min read

Imagine you’re assembling IKEA furniture. You stare at step 14 in the instructions, but can't figure out which screw goes where. The diagram shows something, but it's not clicking. 

If you were to ask: "What's the small metal piece in step 14 for?", a standard text-based retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) system can't help here. The answer lives in the diagram itself, not in any text on the page. IKEA instructions are famously wordless.

This is where multimodal RAG comes in.

title: Advanced RAG Techniques for High-Performance LLM Applications
snippet:
Isobel Krüger

October 17, 2025

17 min read

Image 27: A constellation of Orion represented as a graph with nodes and relationships.

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) enhances Large Language Models (LLMs) by combining retrieval with generation to ground outputs in your own data rather than relying solely on pretraining. In practice, RAG systems retrieve relevant information from a knowledge source and integrate it into the prompt, enabling responses that are more accurate, contextual, and trustworthy. [...] RAG is now a widely used architecture for LLM applications, powering everything from question-answering services that leverage web search, to internal chat tools that index enterprise content, to complex QA pipelines. Its appeal is simple: by augmenting generation with retrieval, teams can deliver LLM experiences that meet today’s expectations for relevance and reliability. [...] Adopt CRAG patterns. It’s essential for high-stakes domains such as legal, compliance, or support deflection with strict SLAs.
   Plan for scale. Use hybrid indices, approximate‑nearest‑neighbor vector search (ANN), and caching of common queries to cut latency without losing quality. Keep evaluations running as you optimize.

Image 32: A diagram showing the steps for implementing advanced RAG, including the baseline setup, improvements, framework integration, and the finished advanced RAG pipeline
### Where to Go From Here

Advanced RAG is about steady, incremental improvements across retrieval, context management, and generation (not a single trick).

title: Exploring the Evolution of Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) | Asit Piri posted on the topic | LinkedIn
snippet: to a retrieval engine (like a vector database) so they can pull in relevant, factual information before generating a response. This creates an AI that can reason with context, not just pattern-match text. 🔹 2. RAG Pipeline Workflow Step 1: Query Understanding & Embedding The user’s question is converted into a vector embedding, a numerical representation of meaning. Step 2: Document Retrieval The embedding is compared to document embeddings stored in a vector database (e.g., Chroma, Weaviate, Pinecone). → The system fetches the top N most semantically similar chunks using cosine similarity. Step 3: Context Augmentation The retrieved chunks are added to the LLM prompt (as “context”), grounding it in real data. Step 4: Response Generation The LLM (e.g., Gemini, GPT, Claude) uses this [...] RAG Is (Still) What You Need — and Here’s the Evidence What RAG Is: Retrieval-Augmented Generation RAG connects language models to external knowledge sources. It first retrieves relevant information from a knowledge base — using embeddings stored in a vector database — and then generates a response grounded in that context rather than relying solely on the model’s internal memory. This retrieval process uses Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) indices to find semantically similar information in milliseconds, even across millions of documents, with minimal loss in precision. The original formulation of RAG (Lewis et al., 2020) demonstrated clear gains in open-domain QA tasks compared to purely parametric models — marking the beginning of a new generation of retrieval-augmented LLMs. 📄 [...] 🤖 Beyond Basics: How RAG Pipelines Make GenAI Smarter and More Reliable After exploring tokenization, chunking, and vector embeddings during my Outamation Externship (facilitated by Kristhia and Satya), I finally got to see how everything comes together through RAG: Retrieval-Augmented Generation. It’s one of the most powerful architectures behind today’s GenAI systems because it allows language models to access external knowledge in real time rather than relying only on what they were trained on. Here’s a breakdown of how RAG pipelines work and why they matter: 🔹 1. The Core Idea Traditional LLMs are “closed book” — they generate text based only on their training data. RAG turns them into “open book” systems by connecting them to a retrieval engine (like a vector database) so they can

title: Agentic RAG
Advanced RAG Techniques: an Illustrated Overview | Towards AI
snippet:
## A comprehensive study of the advanced retrieval augmented generation techniques and algorithms, systemising various approaches. The article comes with a collection of links in my knowledge base referencing various implementations and studies mentioned.

Since the goal of the post is to make an overview & explanation of avaliable RAG algorithms and techniques, I won’t dive into implementations details in code, just referencing them and leaving it to the vast documentation & tutorials available.

## Intro

If you are familiar with the RAG concept, please skip to the Advanced RAG part. [...] Retrieval Augmented Generation, aka RAG, provides LLMs with the information retrieved from some data source to ground its generated answer on. Basically RAG is Search + LLM prompting, where your ask the model to answer the query provided the information found with the search algorithm as a context. Both the query and the retrieved context are injected into the prompt that is sent to the LLM.

RAG is the most popular architecture of the LLM based systems in 2023. There are many products build almost solely on RAG — from Question Answering services combining web search engines with LLMs to hundreds of chat-with-your-data apps. [...] Documents are splitted into an hierarchy of chunks and then the smallest leaf chunks are sent to index. At the retrieval time we retrieve k leaf chunks, and if there is n chunks referring to the same parent chunk, we replace them with this parent chunk and send it to LLM for answer generation.

Fetch smaller chunks during retrieval first, then if more than n chunks in top k retrieved chunks are linked to the same parent node (larger chunk), we replace the context fed to the LLM by this parent node — works like auto merging a few retrieved chunks into a larger parent chunk, hence the method name. Just to note — search is performed just within the child nodes index. Check the LlamaIndex tutorial on Recursive Retriever + Node References for a deeper dive.

title: Advanced RAG: LongRAG, Self-RAG and GraphRAG Explained - Rost Glukhov | Personal site and technical blog
snippet:
Notes on the margins

Rost Glukhov. Personal site and technical blog

# Advanced RAG: LongRAG, Self-RAG and GraphRAG Explained

LongRAG, Self-RAG, GraphRAG - Next-gen techniques

Page content

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has evolved far beyond simple vector similarity search. LongRAG, Self-RAG, and GraphRAG represent the cutting edge of these capabilities.

Modern RAG systems need to handle massive documents, understand complex entity relationships and much more.

This nice image is generated by AI model Flux 1 dev.

## The Evolution Beyond Basic RAG [...] ## Self-RAG: Reflective Retrieval

### Core Principles

Self-RAG introduces a metacognitive layer to RAG systems. Instead of blindly retrieving and generating, the system actively reflects on its own processes through special reflection tokens:

Retrieve Token: Decides whether retrieval is necessary for a given query Relevance Token: Evaluates if retrieved documents are actually relevant Support Token: Checks if the generated answer is supported by the retrieved content Critique Token: Assesses the overall quality of the generated response

### Architecture Components

The Self-RAG architecture consists of three interleaved phases: [...] ## External References

 Microsoft GraphRAG: A Modular Graph-Based Retrieval-Augmented Generation System
 Self-RAG: Learning to Retrieve, Generate, and Critique through Self-Reflection
 LongRAG: Enhancing Retrieval-Augmented Generation with Long-context LLMs

 FAISS: A Library for Efficient Similarity Search
 LangChain Documentation: Advanced RAG Techniques
 HuggingFace: Sentence Transformers for Embedding Models
 RAG Survey: A Comprehensive Analysis of Retrieval Augmented Generation

About Rost Glukhov

Rost Glukhov is a plyglot developer, has a special interest in web, mobile, backend, AI, devops and other areas. For more details have a look at the About page.
